text: The surgeon begins creating a peritoneal flap from lateral to medial, carefully avoiding entry into the retro-rectus space.

The surgeon attempts to develop the peritoneal flap, noting difficulty due to the high positioning. The right side of the orifice is visibly blown out, and the surgeon carefully hugs the peritoneum to find the correct plane.

The surgeon uses the left hand for retraction to expose weak indirect and direct hernia spots, as well as an early developing femoral hernia. The areolar tissue is visible, and the inferior epigastric vessels are identified. Despite staying close to the peritoneum, the surgeon enters the retrorectus space below the arcuate line.

The surgeon attempts to separate the fibroareolar tissue from the peritoneum, working laterally to the epigastric vessels. The transverse fascia is visible, typically associated with direct hernias, as the hernia sac is exposed.

The surgeon carefully dissects the transverse salus fascia away from the hernia contents, maintaining close proximity to the peritoneum to ensure the testicular vessels are separated from the peritoneum. The direct portion of the hernia is visible during this process.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneum from the testicular vessels, carefully avoiding the vessels while working in a broad fashion from left to right. A small accidental hole is visible in the peritoneum, with minimal presence of a hernia sac.

The surgeon begins reducing the femoral hernia, revealing a significant amount of fat within the hernia sac. The fat is distinct from the obturator fat located more to the right, which remains untouched.
The surgeon dissects across the midline, revealing an indentation in the direct space that was previously covered by fat. The removal of the fat exposes the underlying area.

The surgeon carefully separates the bladder from surrounding tissues, maintaining a hemostatic technique to ensure clear visibility of the surgical plane. The peritoneum is hugged closely as the surgeon prioritizes positioning the cord posteriorly to ensure the mesh covers the posterior aspect effectively, preventing recurrence.

The surgeon carefully hugs the peritoneum, leaving a fascial layer on the pelvic nerves, including the lateral cutaneous and femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve. The mesh is positioned to transition from the retro-pubic space into the space of Retzius without bladder or peritoneum interference. Fat is visibly separated from the bladder fat, indicating the desired surgical plane.

The surgeon maintains hemostasis, providing a clear view of the surgical field. The full bladder is visible, aiding in the dissection by highlighting its curvature, which will assist in tucking the mesh later.

The surgeon completes the dissection, ensuring no tissue can sneak under the mesh. The obturator nerve becomes visible, appearing more medial than usual. The dissection reveals a weak medial pubic obturator (MPO) area.

The surgeon prepares to use an extra-large mesh to cover the dissected area. They move to the left side to perform a similar dissection, noting the difference in technique due to being right-handed with the scissors.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument to assist with retraction and separation of tissue planes during flap development. The technique involves staying outside the retro-rectus space and moving laterally to reach the peritoneum. Various microfacial planes are visible, although unnamed, and the surgeon selects the optimal plane for the patient.

The surgeon connects the dissection to the opposite side, focusing on staying close to the peritoneum. The dissection plane remains consistent laterally, facilitating access between the testicular vessels, peritoneum, and hernia sac.

The surgeon observes that the patient is very thin, requiring precise dissection due to minimal fat between tissue planes. The dissection is lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels, revealing the transverse fascia, indicative of a direct inguinal hernia. The hernia is noted to be lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels and connects with the medial part of the hernia. The entire myopectineal orifice (MPO) appears weak, extending beyond the MPO, necessitating reinforcement with an extra-large mesh.

The surgeon exposes the medial aspect of the hernia, revealing the inferior epigastric vessels floating in front of the defect. The hernia appears as a large defect, visually separated in a pantaloon fashion by the inferior epigastric vessels.

The surgeon applies good retraction, pulling the tissue to about eight or nine o'clock to unstack and visualize the layers. The scissor is used like a hot scalpel, utilizing its side to efficiently dissect through the tissue. The internal ring with core structures is visible, distinct from the adjacent defect.

The surgeon attempts to enter the same dissection plane on the left side as previously established on the right, aiming to expose the superior pubic rami. Fat is being carefully removed to reveal the optimal plane, with attention to preserving the obturator lymph nodes, which are visually distinct due to their different color compared to other fat tissues.

The surgeon prioritizes the dissection around the cord, carefully hugging the peritoneum to avoid injuring the autonomic nerves near the vas and testicular vessels. Medial retraction is applied to move tissue away from the vas, iliac vein, obturator artery, nerve, and vein, aiding in finding the correct dissection plane.

The surgeon performs a precise dissection near the obturator nerve, avoiding excessive cautery to minimize bleeding. Despite some bleeding, the surgeon continues to hug the peritoneum, being cautious of the bowel located on the opposite side.

The surgeon navigates around the full bladder, using its convexity as a visual guide during the dissection. The bladder's shape aids in identifying its location, facilitating the robotic tap procedure. The dissection is completed without the use of a Foley catheter.

The surgeon switches from using scissors to a mega suture cut needle driver, preparing to perform suturing tasks. The surgical assistant at the bedside is responsible for handling this instrument change.

The surgeon positions a vertical blue line on the vas deferens as a guide for placing the mesh, which is tucked vertically into the retro pubic space. The distended bladder aids in positioning the mesh, ensuring excellent lateral and posterior overlap. The mesh visibly overlaps by two to three centimeters above the weak, dilated transverse arch, preventing the peritoneum from slipping behind.

The surgeon secures the mesh to Cooper's ligament using a suture to prevent it from lifting, despite its secure placement. An additional stitch is placed medially due to the dilated floor, using a 3-0 Vicryl suture cut to ten inches for multiple uses.

The surgeon efficiently sutures the mesh, tucking it into the retro pubic space to ensure it remains vertical. The vertical line of the mesh is aligned over the vas, providing excellent coverage with no peritoneum behind it. The posterior lateral edge of the mesh is positioned securely against the cord structures.

The surgeon places two stitches on the attenuated posterior rectus sheath to prevent the mesh from rotating clockwise and exiting the pelvis. The stitches are not tightly secured to allow the mesh to adjust its position when the abdomen is desufflated.

The surgeon completes the mesh placement and begins closing the defect using a 3-0 V-Loc 180 suture. The suture is visibly used to close the flap efficiently, with the surgeon preferring a six-inch length for optimal handling and closure success.

The surgeon skillfully maneuvers the needle with the right hand, avoiding dropping it to save time. The left hand pulls the needle out, and the wrist is rotated to present the needle to the right hand, minimizing fumbling. The surgeon double backs two throws to lock the suture in place and cuts the tail without tension to prevent it from springing out. The small hole is fixed using a Vicryl suture.

The surgeon uses a short suture to close the incision from lateral to medial, carefully handling the peritoneal edges without lifting the lower flap too high to avoid tearing. The left hand presents the needle to the right hand, maintaining efficiency with a six-inch V-lock suture, allowing for multiple throws before pulling through. As the pneumoperitoneum pressure is lowered, the surgeon presses up to hold the peritoneum while evacuating gas from the pocket.

The surgeon completes the closure of the peritoneal defect and increases the pneumoperitoneum pressure to fifteen millimeters mercury to verify the mesh placement, ensuring there is no posterior lifting of the mesh.
